text:
Audio: Welcome, date of 100, another content lecture. This is our um... Third lecture of the semester and the second panda's lecture. So what did we see in the previous lecture? In the previous lecture, we went ahead and talked a lot about different tools that you can use in the data science life cycle. What we started talking about was what are different data structures and pandas we saw. We saw what, what is the 2D data structure that we saw in the previous lecture? Data Frame Data frame. What was the two, one, the data structures that we had seen, there was series and there was index object. So we saw how you're connected together. At the end of the lecture, what we started doing was actually data extraction. And I didn't have time to complete that part, so I'm going to continue from where we left off in the previous lecture. We're going to talk about how we can do data extraction using integer position, using square brackets, brackets and then we're going to go a little bit deeper into more fun things. So today is going to be getting more practice in terms of what are additional functionalities that are available in pandas so that we can do different fun things with a data frame. How can we add data? How can we modify data? How can we add rows? And how can we make conditional selection? Video: 1: Lec 03 - DS100 Fa24 - Pandas || X

text:
Audio: So we're going to go a little bit. Deeper into pandas and a little bit of a content warning for today is that there's gonna be a lot of different functionalities Of pandas coming your way so again, it might sound overwhelming because we're covering too much But you don't need to memorize everything as long as you remember the logic of how to use this stuff and how to make sure that There is a function that does this certain thing so that you don't need to loop over the entire data frame that you have the whole The whole purpose of us going through this API is that you know that everything can be handled in a vectorized way and you don't need to actually loop any time during any of the homeworks and projects that you're working on. So just knowing that those functionalities exist is the job of us in today's lecture and then we're going to go a little bit deeper into more complex stuff next week on Tuesday but then you have the weekend to just get some rest and then have all of this content make sense to you before you come back on Tuesday. So let's dive in with what we wrapped up the previous lecture. Remember the lock operator lock. You can think about it as label based extraction that we're doing. So we said that lock this L. You can remember it as is working. Um You can think about lock as label-based extraction. And you will see later why I keep like highlighting that L in lock, because you will see eye lock. So with lock we're doing label-based extraction. What was label? Label is all of those bolded text that you see on all of the rows and all of the columns. Where are they? So here we have all of the labels on these rows, and we have all of the labels on all of these columns. Labels are the ways that we are referring to each one of those columns and rows. We can manually provide label, we can manually provide label and index different rows that we have, or we could actually not provide names and labels and then pandas is going to automatically label stuff for us. So for example here, all of the labels are manually provided. Video: 1: Lec 03 - DS100 Fa24 - Pandas || X

So you can provide the list of rows, the list of column labels, and then it will do the extraction for you. We talked about we can do the same extraction using a slice, right? So with the slice, we're providing a range of rows and a range of columns, and then it will do the extraction for us. There was one major thing that I wanted you to remember from the previous lecture was that when we're providing a slice to the lock operator, it is inclusive of the right hand side, which is unusual with pandas, right? Or python, generally. So it's always exclusive of the right hand side. This is the only place that is inclusive of the right hand side. text:
Audio: We can provide a list. We can provide this list. Now it's list is working nicely similar to what we're expecting in Python. that it is exclusive of the right-hand side and then a single value. So let's see some examples. If I want to provide a list, so I have to provide a list for both rows and the columns. So what is the list of rows that I'm providing and providing the list of integer position 1, 2, and 3? So these are the rows that are integer position 1, 2, and 3. And it happens that integer position aligns with the label. Again, it's because our integer position is starched from 0. And then the automatic indexing also started initially automatically labeled at zero. So it happens that when it is automatically labeled, the integer position and label values are going to be exactly this thing. So don't confuse that. And then if you're saying that okay one column at integer position 0, 1 and 2, again indexing or integer position starts from 0, that means that column 1, column 2 and column 3, or the first column, second column, and the third column that you're seeing here that. The first column was here, second column was candidate, and the third column was party. Video: 1: -

text:
Audio: So the intersection of these two is going to be extracted for us using the two list items that we provide. One reminder from previous lecture, we said that all these labels or the items that you're extracting and the rows are mandatory. So you have to provide what you want to extract. If you don't want to extract or if you don't want to filter the rows, you have to specify that I'm extracting all. So you provide the column and then you provide whatever list of columns that you want to extract. Here is also the same thing with everything that we're covering. It's that either we explicitly say that I want all of the rows by providing column, because that's also another way that we can specify that I want everything starting from the beginning up until the end. Or you provide the list of or the list of or the single row that you want to extract. And you can't skip rows. But you can skip columns. So here I can skip columns and then say that, okay, row at integer position 1, 2 and 3, but then I don't want to provide a selection on the number under columns and it will automatically assume that you want all and it will extract all for you. So this is the difference between how we treat rows and columns. So this again works similarly between lock and unlock operator. Video: 1: -

Audio: So here I'm saying that row at integer position. Zera, which is the first row and then the second column at integer position 1 is the name column so it happens that in 1824 Andrew Jackson was the first candidate that was listed in our data frame and that's what is going to be returned and the data type is obviously going to be string because that's the value that we're extracted. So luck, we talk about I like why do we care about them so much on which one should be used. Again remember luck was label-based extraction. I like is integer-based extraction. Which one is easier to use? Most of the time you will use law. So you will lose lock because it's easier to make sense of columns. Instead of thinking about which column was candidate column and start counting from zero and this is integer position two or not, you can directly say candidate, go ahead and go ahead and find it and then grab me the candidate column. And that would be much easier. So a lot of time you will use lock again because it makes more sense. And also a lot of times... It is more readable, right? So not only that, that it's easier, that you don't need to inspect your data frame before writing code, it is also easier. So you can directly write, for example, in this case, that I'm doing label-based extraction, grabbing all of the rows and then grabbing year and candidate. So that's the way of doing it with labels, but with the, sorry, here is also extracting results. But then instead if you wanted to do it eye lock, you should do exactly the same thing. Zera, which is the first row and then the second column at integer position 1 is the name column so it happens that in 1824 Andrew Jackson was the first candidate that was listed in our data frame and that's what is going to be returned and the data type is obviously going to be string because that's the value that we're extracted. So luck, we talk about I like why do we care about them so much on which one should be used. Again remember luck was label-based extraction. I like is integer-based extraction. Which one is easier to use? Most of the time you will use law. So you will lose lock because it's easier to make sense of columns. Instead of thinking about which column was candidate column and start counting from zero and this is integer position two or not, you can directly say candidate, go ahead and go ahead and find it and then grab me the candidate column. And that would be much easier. So a lot of time you will use lock again because it makes more sense. And also a lot of times... It is more readable, right? So not only that, that it's easier, that you don't need to inspect your data frame before writing code, it is also easier. So you can directly write, for example, in this case, that I'm doing label-based extraction, grabbing all of the rows and then grabbing year and candidate. So that's the way of doing it with labels, but with the, sorry, here is also extracting results. But then instead if you wanted to do it eye lock, you should do exactly the same thing.

text:
Audio: So that's why a lot of time when we're talking about using a slice, we would say that we want to slice the row numbers, not row labels. Right? When we want to extract a number of features, say I'm interested in candidate and I'm interested in results that I see who won the election and who lost the election. There are two things that I want to extract and two columns that I want to extract. So every time that we are going to look at a list, we're looking at a list of column labels. Probably you care less about providing list of rows, right? You'd want probably one have a row 10 or a row, for example, 20 and then 25. That's like very unusual and not frequently used. But it is very frequently used that you want to extract a certain number of columns with the label. And also the same thing with a single column, a single label. One is for the less one is for the context dependent extraction and what's happening is that it's gonna go ahead grab all the rows because you didn't care about the rows and then you need to You specify that you won't hear candidate and result So a question for you. What happens here if I go ahead and say, okay, elections? And then I would say this is a square bracket notation and I would say that okay, let me try give the labels for the rows and I would go ahead and say I won these three rows. One, two. Sorry, one, two, and three. What's going to happen there? Make a wide guess. That was a good guess, another one. Anyone else? Yes. the rose one to tree. So the trick here, but contacts dependent extraction, or this is square bracket notation, is that if you provide anything that's not a label in your column, it will just go ahead and it will try to do the key extraction or key lookup in a dictionary. Remember that our columns were dictionary? It will say that, okay, you give me key one. Let me go ahead and see if you have that key in your dictionary of columns. and it will go ahead and search over all of the columns, even find it, it will throw a key error. So you won't get anything. So that's why it's context dependent. And that's why it's really important that you know the use case of each one of them. Video: 1: V

text:
Audio: Sorry, the poll was changed after I created the code. But you get the sense of why you might get here, how to use log, how to use i log, and then how to use integer-based extraction and whatnot. So on to the next topic. So you might be asking me that, now I guess, we're interested in like doing label-based extraction, integer-based extraction or everything. But what if there is a condition that I want to select rows or columns based on, or values based on a certain condition? We have seen this in a series. So in the series scenario we said that we can use Boolean expression in order to say that okay. The rows that would correspond are the elements that have the Boolean value corresponding to true are going to be included in the output and then everything with false is going to be excluded from the output. The way that we can use it is that we can use the same kind of scenario that we have all of those Boolean values and we can provide those Boolean values as indices to the rows and then do the extraction using either context dependent extraction, square brackets, or dot block. And we're going to take a look at that. And again, all the Res with True Valley are going to be included and I'll put everything else is going to be excluded. So in this case, I'm going to switch to a new dataset. So this is a new dataset of baby names. This is the dataset from social security that has the name of the babies who were born in California and the original dataset has all these dates but we're looking at only California from different from different years you're seeing and then the state we have here we have also the name of those babies and we have the count of how many babies with that a specific name was registered and then we also have the sign sex at birth that is also reported on those babies so this is kind of an aggregate data set and multiple different features and then they're counting how many of those occurrences of those combinations we have of go over the rest of the topics here, I'm going to not look at the entire dataset but I'm doing a selection of only the top 10 roads. So I call the top 10 roads to be baby names. Video: 1: -

audio: Sorry, the poll was changed after I created the code. But you get the sense of why you might get here, how to use log, how to use i log, and then how to use integer-based extraction and whatnot. So on to the next topic. So you might be asking me that, now I guess, we're interested in like doing label-based extraction, integer-based extraction or everything. But what if there is a condition that I want to select rows or columns based on, or values based on a certain condition? We have seen this in a series. So in the series scenario we said that we can use Boolean expression in order to say that okay. The rows that would correspond are the elements that have the Boolean value corresponding to true are going to be included in the output and then everything with false is going to be excluded from the output. The way that we can use it is that we can use the same kind of scenario that we have all of those Boolean values and we can provide those Boolean values as indices to the rows and then do the extraction using either context dependent extraction, square brackets, or dot block. And we're going to take a look at that. And again, all the Res with True Valley are going to be included and I'll put everything else is going to be excluded. So in this case, I'm going to switch to a new dataset. So this is a new dataset of baby names. This is the dataset from social security that has the name of the babies who were born in California and the original dataset has all these dates but we're looking at only California from different from different years you're seeing and then the state we have here we have also the name of those babies and we have the count of how many babies with that a specific name was registered and then we also have the sign sex at birth that is also reported on those babies so this is kind of an aggregate data set and multiple different features and then they're counting how many of those occurrences of those combinations we have of go over the rest of the topics here, I'm going to not look at the entire dataset but I'm doing a selection of only the top 10 roads. So I call the top 10 roads to be baby names.

So this ampersand here is going to be an amp operator and then this vertical bar here is going to be our OR operator. So we can do AND and OR also on the expression. So you will have two separate conditions. You combine them using AND OR and hopefully you know what the result of AND OR. is when you are applying it to two different conditions. audio: Right, so this is like a very powerful way of doing, of us doing the filtering based on the based on different conditions. Obviously we could do exactly the same thing with lock and then also say that okay now we are going to get all of the columns or a selection of columns and so on and so forth so it would basically filter it for us in the same base. So here's the condition that the silver filtering based on the sex of the baby being equal to F. So one thing that you might be wondering is that, okay, what happens if I don't have a very straightforward condition such as like a name being Joey or like the sex being a certain thing or not, but if I have multiple conditions, we can create compound Boolean expressions as well, using bitwise operators. So this ampersand here is going to be an amp operator and then this vertical bar here is going to be our OR operator. So we can do AND and OR also on the expression. So you will have two separate conditions. You combine them using AND OR and hopefully you know what the result of AND OR. is when you are applying it to two different conditions. So what's happening in this case? Let's take a look. So I have a data frame baby names. I'm doing a selection, so that's my selection operator. And what's inside that's being selected or filtering my data is actually two Boolean expressions that are or together. So there's or between two expressions. What are those expressions?

text:
Audio: So these are all the female baby names. And then the second condition is that the year be less than 2000. So if either of those options are true that the entry is going to be in the output and you're going to see it here that we have gated some of the conditions that the sex column is f or the sex column is n but the year is before 2000 and all of those are selected. So you can get creative, write different conditions the different way. only take away or like a Why is point that I want to tell you is that make sure that you're always including all of those Boolean expressions that you're writing in parenthesis so that like they don't get mixed up and you don't need to deal with the operator precedence and whatnot and remembering that. So in this case it's super easy and clean and you first Calculate those two expressions then you take or and then everything is clean. There are different bitwise operators. We can indicate... Ebullian expression. So say for example if you have a bullion Valley or a bullion array that's P you can negate it using this tilde So you can say tilde P if it's true it's gonna be false if it's false it's gonna be true Or, and we already talked about it, and this is a carrot operator, not carrot vegetable, this is carrot C-A-R-E-T, and this is bitwise, this is exclusive or. So it's either piece true or Q is true and not both. So when both are true or both are false, the output is going to be false, but this is exclusive or between P and Q. And you might want to use it in different places. But this is kind of a basic set of the stuff that you need in order to write any condition that you want. Then you can do you can do extracts based on a certain condition that you have. Video: 1: Lec 03 - DS100 Fa24 - Pandas || X

Audio: This last one is very similar. So there's lock, there is the same condition, there is the same list of columns that we're extracting, but we're doing dot i-lock here. So this is how like you can in practice you'll keep like applying these functions back to back and keep like filtering in multiple different levels. So that's pretty normal that I have this line line of code. But then i'm like zero to tree. that get me all of the columns. I'm happy with getting all of the columns because right now my columns are already name and count. So that's the good part. But the issue is with the i-lock doing the range of 0 to 2. And i-lock was exclusive of the right hand side. So meaning that here is going to give me row at integer position 1 and 2. And that's about it. Right. So this in this case you're seeing that I don't get enough rows unless the issue. But otherwise mostly was under right track. So, and you got it right. And moving on. So I'm going to now go deeper into a lot of different functions. So every slide, almost from this point on, is going to be a new function. It's not going to be logic. So it's just throwing different functions and different kind of options at you. Video: 1: V

text:
Audio: So say I want to write a condition, I want to compound this condition that I want to select names that not only Joey but I want to get like Bella Alex the least on Narrages and say that this list can be long. So it's really hard to write the condition and like, spell out all of those different options that you have, but Pandas has different kinds of easier way of implementing similar stuff. So the first function that I'm going to show you is dot is in. So dot is in the, as you can see from the name, and pandas is really readable, and the API is really kind of... Velh, chosen those function names really Velh, it basically says that it's going to check whether something is in something else, right? So how can I make use of this? The way that I can make use of this is that I can say that I'm going to put all of those names that I wanted, remember Bella, Alex, Nargis and Lisa, into a list. And then what I can do is I can go ahead and say that on the baby names. Name column on that series object Check whether those elements are in The name's the list that I have. So very simple, the output is going to be, again, the inner side is going to basically check whether it is in or not, true false, true false, true false, and then I can pass it to the context dependent extraction. This is square bracket operator and then it will do the actual task of filtering for us. So that's also another easier, higher level way of checking condition or creating more complex condition. text:
Audio: So there is a whole set of functions that are available in this third library that we can use and we're going to go over them later. So you might be asking that you are talking about selection, you're talking about filtering and whatnot, you did not tell us how we can modify it. So if someone hands us a data, we're just basically limited to just filtering it and just taking a look at the filtered version. You didn't talk about how we can modify it. And in fact, there are different easy ways to modify a data frame. We can easily add a column. So adding a column is as easy as adding a key value pair in a dictionary. So how did you add a key value pair in a dictionary? You would just automatically say that I have this key and then provide the value. So it works exactly the same way here. We would say that I am creating this new column. Just provide the key, provide the name. And I'm saying that the values that you're going to store in this column is going to be coming from this series object. So you have to provide kind of an array type value of the same length as the length of the initial data frame and then it will automatically append that to the entire data frame that you have. text:
Audio: So in the same way, we can do exactly the same thing. We can say that, okay, now, remember that I had this Name Lenses. Call them. Go ahead and grab the value for it from the value that I already had just reduced it by one. So again, every time that you're referring to a column like this. If it doesn't exist, it will create one. It will assign the value from whatever you're providing in the right hand side. If it does exist, whatever you're providing in the right hand side is going to overwrite its existing content. Right? So that's exactly how dictionaries work. And this is exactly how it works in pandas. So here all of the name blanks is reduced by one and you can verify because mere is for characters and now it says trees so we correctly modified it. What if I give you a data frame or you create a name for a data frame or a column and you're not happy with the name that we gave to that column. Maybe the label is too long. You don't want to keep typing name, underscore, and length. And you want to rename it to something else. You can use dot rename and you can provide a dictionary that dictionary is structured as all of those previous names or old names that you had and then the corresponding current name that you want to assign it to. So for example here I would say that okay I have baby names, I want to rename it and then and what I'm renaming is that I'm renaming columns. Video: 1: v

text:
Audio: And then the previous value that I had for one column was named underscore Lenses and the new value that I want to sign or the new label is going to be Lenses. or likes. And you can rename multiple columns at the same time. So you just add another element to this dictionary, and then you should be able to rename multiple columns all at once. In this case, the output is just going to be that now I have length column instead of... Name underscored lenses. Okay, what if I want to drop a row or I want to drop a colon? By default, pandas things that you want to drop a row. So you call .drop. It will go ahead and drop a row for you. And you will specify which row you want to drop. If you want to drop a column, you should specifically say that the axis that I'm dropping is the column axis. And then this way you can drop a column. So again, you can drop a row, you can drop a column, and this is a way that you can remove content from your data frame. So here I would say .drop. And I'm saying that the label that you're dropping is lengths. Remember that this was a column. text:
Audio: But let's take a look at them quickly. If I have a data frame when I say dot size, that dot shape, it will give you the number of rows. and it will give you the number of columns. Right? So that's easy for you to figure out how many rows do you have if you want to provide this slice and like get this slice and making sure that you're within the range. So that's an easy way to get the dimensions or have a sense of the dimensions or the size of the data. If you're interested in just knowing how many entries could possibly be in your data frame, you could just call that size. That size is technically the capacity of the data frame. It's number of rows times number of columns. And if you don't trust me, if you multiply 5 by that 400,000 thing, this is the number that you'll get. Video: 1: Lec 03 - DS100 Fa24 - Pandas || X

So when we are doing visualization, we want to visualize distributions that's going to be fun time, that we can visualize mid-max on all the quartets. If I call dot describe on a series object, so here I'm getting the series object only one column and just calling the dot describe in this case, but I have is Something is slightly different, but it just describes something and that's how pandas is implemented So what you're seeing here What you are seeing? Okay, this is a work. time maybe. It tells me how many elements I have. It still gives me count. It tells me how many unique elements I got there. And then it will tell me what is the top frequency of those unique things that I have. We know that it's F and M. And it tells me that F or female babies are more frequent in the data frame. And then it will tell me that the frequency of female babies is this much. So that's how it's implemented. We can also sample from a data frame. So there is a whole set of lectures that we're going to talk about sampling a data frame and just looking at the distribution and whatnot. So in order to sample data from a data frame, we can use .sample. So very creative. Without sample it always assumes that sampling is without replacement. So there is a chance that you sample multiple times back to back and then you will be able to sample the same thing or you sample five entries and then you get one entry twice. Video: 1: V

That means that you won't get the same int or same same data multiple times. but you can set that replace equal to true so you make it with replacement, which in that case you should be able to extract same entry multiple points. Then you can also apply a lock and I like and all those things on top of that and then get more useful information. So if I call dot sample randomly we'll give me one sample. If I call dot sample 5 and provide an argument here this means that I've won five elements. So sample 5 entries for me. And then on top of that, I'm saying that, okay, now that you gave me this data frame, but five samples, I want to index into it. I want to index into it and then say that I want all the rows, so keep all of those five entries that you gave me, and then give me columns, starting from column at integer position 2 all the way to the end. Which technically means that if this is 0, this is 1, this is 2 all the way to the end means year, name and count are going to be included as you're seeing here. We can go more complex and keep adding stuff on top of each other. Video: 1: Lec 03 - DS100 Fa24 - Pandas || X

text:
Audio: I can say that let's first go ahead and filter these babies so that I'm only looking at the interest in the year 2000, right? And then sample from the babies in the year 2000. So a lot of time when we tell you a certain condition, you need to think about what makes sense and what order you should apply stuff, right? So that you're sampling from the right distribution. So if you were like doing the filtering afterwards. Then your sampling was not going to be correct. So here in order to make sure that my sampling frame is correct, I'm filtering based on getting the year equal to 2000. Then what I'm doing is that I'm saying that sample for instances would for me and then replacement is allowed in this case. So it does it with replacement. After you're done, after you gave me four samples from the year 2000, then I'm doing an additional thing where I'm saying that, Give me all of those rows, but only give me the same columns, the year name and count, and this is how it's going to look like. Audio: Okay, so we can keep adding these functions together. I think I've sped it enough. You know what? We do it. Value accounts. So a value account applies to series objects. So when we're calling value accounts on a specific column, the way that it will work is that it will calculate The frequency of each unique occurrences in your in-dad the specific series object. So for example when I say name.value accounts, it will basically go ahead and calculate or find out all of the unique names that we have. So these are all unique. You can see it two rows with the same name. And then it will tell me that what is the frequency of each one of them, each one of those values? There is a note that this frequency thing is different than count, right? Every row which had a count is counted as one occurrence. And then we're just counting how many times they appear, which technically here means that I think it means that how many years was it that a baby with this name? BuzzForn. Right? Kind of sort of. because that 223 is a very large number. But anyhow, we can also be seen unique, unique as just that, like if you don't care about the frequencies and you care about the values, you should just call unique and then it will give you an array with all of those unique names. So that's what I did when I wanted to make sure that there is a true instance there that the babies our name Joy for Frank and then we moved on. Video: 1: Lec 03 - DS100 Fa24 - Pandas || X

Audio: So a lot of time if you want to do something like that. And if they tell you that the data is sorted, you just sorted again, don't trust them. Or if it's not sorted, you still sorted and do your own thing. So, sort value, when it's called on series, it's very straightforward. It will automatically sort all of those values based on the expectation of how sorting works for that data type. So for example, for it, a string sorting works in alphabetical ordering. So all of those letters are going to be ordered alphabetically in alphabetical ordering. So here sort values you see, we're starting from Aidan and then go all the way to the names that are starting with Z. So by default the order is in ascending order, not in the sending order. And if you want the order to be descending you have to set the Set the parameter that you have say ascending false so that it source them descendingly. So if I want to say that I want to sort my data frame based on the links, but I can do is that go ahead, create a column, name links, and calculate all of those name links values. How do I do that? You had seen it before. I said that go ahead, grab the name column, assume that it's a string, calculate the the length of the story in a series object. Now we run ahead and create it. and then assign those values to that column and then here we're just sorting the data frame by that column that we created. Very simple. So this is like for you to think about what functionalities did you know and then how can you create them? And then once you're done with this column you can drop it afterwards. Once you sort of everything you can drop that column and then reassign the values. We can also use a key argument. So here we would say that I want you to sort values and I'm telling you that sort by name. But don't start by name based on the data type of a string that sort's based on alphabetical ordering or the encoding of those strings. But instead I want you to sort it based on this key argument. That Lambda function says that... Once you have a string, once you have a series object, what you're going to do is that assume that it's a string, get the lengths, and that's going to be the priority or order of this string value or that entry. And then it will basically try to sort the entire data frame by the name using the priority that this key argument provided. So think about key as providing the priority that overrides the initial data type priority. And the last fancy way of doing it, which I think is going to have appear in only one of the homeworks, but it will appear somewhere that you don't expect. And then sort names based on the frequency of the RNA in that, which is kind of crazy, but it's kind of a made up thing to go over this complicated example. So I'm creating this column. I'm saying that the values in this column should be calculated using the name column, initial name, and then what I'll say is that go ahead and use all of those values and apply the function we are underscored EA Count. What is that function? That function basically says that, okay, when you get on a string here, go ahead and count dr, go ahead and count EA, return that value. So what it does, that line of code, is that every instance in each one of the rows, it will go ahead, get that a string, pass it to the function, map that function into that string, Get the corresponding value, store that value into the column that I created.
